result:
If I could change the world in one way, it would be to alter our perception of time.

Not with a device, but as an innate, fundamental sense. I would stretch the human sense of the "present" to encompass a hundred years—fifty years into the past and fifty years into the future.

We wouldn't just intellectually know that our grandparents were young or that our grandchildren will exist; we would *feel* it. The consequences of our actions today would not be an abstract problem for a future generation, but an immediate, tangible reality.

A forest cleared would feel like a lung being removed from our own body. A law passed that indebts the young would feel like stealing from our own dinner table tonight. The wisdom of an elder would be as relevant as this morning's news.

This single shift wouldn't eliminate greed or anger or fear. But it would re-contextualize them. Ambition would be forced to have vision. Apathy would become an act of self-harm. We would cease to be fleeting visitors on this planet and would instead become its long-term custodians, because the future would no longer be someone else's problem. It would be ours, viscerally and inescapably.

It's not about living longer. It's about living deeper in the river of time, feeling the currents of what came before and where we are heading, all at once.
